data.title: Estée Lauder faces a lawsuit over alleged tech secrets theft
data.description:
Beauty tech startup Nomi Beauty has filed a lawsuit against Estée Lauder (EL) in federal court in New York City. In the complaint, Nomi Beauty  alleged that the cosmetic product giant stole its hotel‑focused technology and trade secrets and effectively drove the startup out of business.

Lawyers for Nomi said the New York-based company shared proprietary methods as parts of agreements inked in 2018 and 2020 for figuring out what products customers truly prefer rather than what they say they want. Estée Lauder (EL) is accused of then terminating those contracts and launching its own competing hotel and travel‑retail programs in markets including China, Costa Rica, Malaysia, the U.K., and the U.S., using similar concepts and know‑how. Nomi claims Estée Lauder (EL) generated “literally billions” in new revenue while modernizing its sales channels and reducing dependence on brick‑and‑mortar stores. At the same time, Estée Lauder (EL) was accused of starving Nomi’s hotel partners of product supply and undermining its business, contributing to its collapse.

Nomi is seeking unspecified compensatory, punitive, and treble damages for trade‑secret theft and related misconduct. Estée Lauder (EL) has not yet provided a detailed public response to the allegations in the lawsuit.

Nomi Beauty's stated mission is to bring the best of what consumers love about Amazon, Sephora, and Duty Free America into the $30B+ travel beauty retail market.

data.title: Adobe trades in red for the seventh straight session
data.description:
Adobe (ADBE) shares fell 1.35% to $292.13 in the afternoon trade on Tuesday, extending losses for a seventh consecutive session.

The stock fell 11.33% between January 9 and January 16, as compared with a 0.38% decline in the S&P 500 over the same period.

Adobe shares have come under pressure following a series of broker downgrades that highlighted rising competition, slowing growth and a lack of near-term catalysts.

BMO Capital Markets cut the stock to Market Perform, citing increasing competitive threats in the creative software market and survey data showing wider adoption of rival Canva among students and freelancers. Jefferies also downgraded Adobe to Hold, saying it has not seen any clear revenue contribution from the company’s artificial intelligence tools. Oppenheimer later downgraded the stock to Perform, pointing to decelerating Digital Media growth, inconsistent execution, durability concerns around Adobe’s moat and lower year-on-year operating margin guidance for fiscal 2026.

The downgrades came as broader weakness weighed on enterprise software stocks, with investors growing cautious about how artificial intelligence could disrupt traditional software business models.

Reports said renewed AI-related fears have driven sharp declines across the software sector, with Adobe and peers falling alongside other enterprise names. Commentators said many incumbents have yet to show meaningful revenue acceleration from their AI initiatives, leaving sentiment weak and near-term upside limited despite lower valuations.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system, Adobe is rated Hold, with a score of 2.94 out of 5, with grades of A+ in profitability and B- in valuation, but a C in revisions, D in growth and D+ in momentum.

While Seeking Alpha analysts suggest buying the stock.

Turning to Wall Street, 21 out of 40 analysts rate the stock Buy or higher, 14 suggest Hold, while five rate it a Strong Sell.

An analyst said recent share price declines have made Adobe attractive for growth-focused investors, citing continued double-digit growth and resilient profitability despite near-term margin pressure. They said, “the most recent growth figures and the forecasted growth for the coming year are still appealing,” adding that Adobe “keeps innovating, and the traction seems to be real across each of the segments in all geographies,” while arguing the stock is trading “at a significant discount compared to its 5-year historic averages.”

Shares have fallen 18.14% over the past month and are down 16.67% year to date.

data.title: Agilent, Danaher could be potential buyers of Qiagen - analyst
data.description:
Agilent Technologies (A) and Danaher (DHR) screen as potential buyers of Qiagen (QGEN) after a report that the European molecular testing firm is weighing its options after takeover interest.

"QGEN would strategically fit into either DHR's or A's portfolio," JPMorgan analyst Casey Woodring wrote in a note on Tuesday. 

Thermo Fisher (TMO) is unlikely to reconsider revisiting its initial bid, according to Woodring.  In August 2020, Thermo Fisher (TMO) walked away from a €43 per share offer for diagnostic test maker Qiagen (QGEN). Thermo Fisher first announced a €39/share offer for QGEN in March 2020, then upped its bid after institutional investors rebelled over the price.

Qiagen (QGEN) has held discussions with several possible bidders in recent weeks, including some US strategic buyers, Bloomberg reported on Tuesday.

Shares of Qiagen jumped 19% on news of the takeover interest.

data.title: NovaBay plunges on sales agreement for $100M stock
data.description:
NovaBay Pharmaceuticals (NBY) lost ~54% on Tuesday after the wound care product maker announced an at-the-market sales agreement with Virtu Americas, an institutional brokerage firm, to sell up to $100M worth of its common stock.

As part of the agreement signed on Jan. 20, NovaBay (NBY) can offer and sell shares of its common stock at a par value of $0.01 per share, with Virtu serving as its sales agent or principal.

Sales can be made from time to time either through Virtu or to Virtu, the Emeryville, California-based pharma company said in a regulatory filing on Tuesday.

As part of other financial terms of the deal, Virtu is entitled to receive a commission of up to 2.0% of the gross proceeds generated from any sale of common stock it has sold under the sales agreement.

Separately on Friday, NovaBay (NBY) announced securities purchase agreements with multiple investors to sell pre-funded warrants that allow the purchase of approximately 837.7M shares of its common stock at a par value of $0.01 per share, generating nearly $134M in gross proceeds.

Update 1.48PM ET: Adds details about the securities purchase agreements

data.title: Mercantile Bank outlines 5% to 7% loan growth target for 2026 while integrating Eastern Michigan acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Mercantile Bank Corporation (MBWM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Raymond Reitsma, President and CEO, highlighted the completion of the Eastern Michigan Bank acquisition on December 31, 2025, stating this move supports the company's strategic objectives around deposit and loan growth, margin stability, asset quality, and financial performance.
  • CEO Reitsma reported, "Over the last 5 quarters, the SOFR 90-day average rates dropped 68 basis points, while our margin increased by 2 basis points to 3.43%." He emphasized a strong net interest margin, low levels of past due loans at 11 basis points, and a loan-to-deposit ratio of 91% at year-end 2025, improved from 98% a year prior.
  • Reitsma explained the company achieved a compounded annual deposit growth rate of 9.2% and loan growth rate of 8.6% over the last five years, while commitments to make loans totaled $297 million and commitments to make commercial and residential construction loans reached $271 million at year-end—both noted as historically high levels.
  • The CEO said, "Growth in commercial deposit relationships has supported growth in treasury management services, resulting in a 19% increase in service charges on accounts during 2025." Payroll services grew by 14%, and mortgage banking income rose by 6% year-over-year.
  • Reitsma summarized, "These trades have allowed us to report a year-over-year EPS growth rate of 11%, 1.4% return on average assets, a 14.1% return on average equity for 2025 and 11% increase in tangible book value per share over the last 4 quarters."
  • Charles Christmas, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, stated, "This morning, we announced net income of $22.8 million or $1.40 per diluted share for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared with net income of $19.6 million or $1.22 per diluted share for the fourth quarter of 2024."
  • Christmas noted, "Interest income on loans declined during the fourth quarter and all of 2025 compared to the prior year period, reflecting a lower yield on loans that was not fully mitigated by loan growth." He also highlighted a decrease of 32 basis points in the cost of deposits for the quarter, and a 2 basis point increase in net interest margin to 3.43%.

Outlook

  • The company projects loan growth in the range of 5% to 7% annualized during each quarter of 2026, reflecting a strong commercial loan pipeline and expected payoffs in the coming months.
  • Christmas stated, "We are forecasting our first quarter 2026 net interest margin to increase from the fourth quarter of 2025 net interest margin, in large part reflecting the Eastern acquisition and further, steady increases throughout the year."
  • The company is forecasting a federal tax rate of 17% for 2026 and expects continued investments to support expansion in Southeast Michigan and operational enhancements from a core and digital banking provider switch.

Financial Results

  • Net income for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $22.8 million or $1.40 per diluted share, up from the same quarter in 2024. Full-year net income totaled $88.8 million or $5.47 per diluted share, compared to $79.6 million or $4.93 per diluted share for 2024.
  • Average loans during Q4 2025 were $4.63 billion, up $62 million year-over-year, while average deposits were $4.83 billion, up $302 million.
  • Net interest income increased $2.7 million in Q4 2025 year-over-year. The reserve for credit losses stood at 1.21% of total loans at year-end 2025.
  • Noninterest expenses rose by $2.9 million in Q4 and $10.2 million for the year, mainly due to higher salaries, benefits, and data processing costs. Acquisition-related costs for Eastern Michigan totaled $1.2 million in Q4 and $1.8 million for the year.
  • The effective tax rates were about 12% for Q4 and 14% for the year, reflecting federal tax credit impacts.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, CGS International: Sought clarification on margin guidance and the impact of purchase accounting accretion. Charles Christmas confirmed, "The purchase accounting in the loan portfolio is about $125,000 net per quarter," and expects a margin in the low to mid-3.4% range, rising to 3.55% to 3.65% in Q1 2026, largely due to the Eastern acquisition.
  • Tamayo asked about noninterest-bearing deposit assumptions. Christmas explained, "The assumption with noninterest balances is... probably a 6% growth in noninterest balances."
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Inquired about using Eastern's excess liquidity and its effect on margin. Christmas stated, "We're definitely using part of it, but from a functionality standpoint, it's hard to use all of it."
  • Nosal asked about Southeast Michigan team additions. Reitsma responded, "We've added a lending team down there that has gotten off to a very nice start... and we continue to be in the market for more lending talent."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Questioned expense guidance and cost saves from the Eastern acquisition. Christmas said, "Most of the cost saves... are more of a 2027 event," with 2026 reflecting continued personnel investments.
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked about optimism on loan growth and capital management. Reitsma attributed confidence to a strong backlog and high payoffs expected to moderate later in 2026. Christmas mentioned a "stronger appetite" for share buybacks in 2026.
  • John Rodis, Janney: Clarified expense guidance and cost saves. Christmas confirmed minimal cost saves in 2026 and anticipated "meaningful reductions in the data processing area" in 2027.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive but probing tone, focusing on margin guidance, use of acquisition liquidity, expense projections, and the sustainability of projected growth. Their questioning indicated cautious optimism but pressed for clarity on cost saves and integration impacts.
  • Management's tone was confident and detailed in prepared remarks, emphasizing stability and execution of strategy. In the Q&A, management was factual, occasionally emphasizing discipline and prudence: "We, here, firmly don't believe in anything... but also interest rates is that hope is not a strategy."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained consistently focused on integration, growth, and cost control, while management's confidence level held steady, supported by strong financial results and completed acquisition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured the completed acquisition of Eastern Michigan Bank, compared to the prior quarter's anticipation of the deal closing.
  • Guidance for loan growth remained in the 5% to 7% range, with management now expecting the impact of elevated payoffs to persist into early 2026 but expressing confidence in the backlog.
  • Tangible book value growth and EPS growth rates were highlighted as strong in both quarters, but the fourth quarter reflected an actualized 11% EPS growth for 2025.
  • Analysts in both quarters concentrated on margin sustainability, expense management, and the timeline for realizing cost saves from the acquisition.
  • Management's tone and strategic focus were stable, with an added emphasis this quarter on early integration progress and plans for expansion in Southeast Michigan.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued elevated commercial loan payoffs as an ongoing risk, expecting them to settle down over 2026.
  • The company acknowledged the challenge of integrating Eastern Michigan Bank, noting early-stage cultural alignment and ongoing operational integration.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the timing of cost saves from the acquisition, with management clarifying that significant savings are not expected until 2027.
  • Market volatility and uncertainty in the interest rate environment were mentioned, but management emphasized a strategy designed to insulate the bank from rate changes.

Final Takeaway

Mercantile Bank Corporation closed 2025 with strong financial results and the successful integration of Eastern Michigan Bank underway. Management projects 5% to 7% annualized loan growth in 2026, supported by a robust commercial pipeline and disciplined expense management. While cost savings from the acquisition are mostly expected in 2027, the company anticipates incremental improvements in net interest margin and ongoing investments to expand its Southeast Michigan presence. Management remains confident in delivering stable performance and capitalizing on future growth opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pfizer exits GSK majority-owned ViiV Healthcare; Shionogi raises stake
data.description:
• Pfizer (PFE) has decided to divest its equity holding in ViiV Healthcare, an HIV treatment maker majority owned by GSK (GSK), while Japanese drugmaker Shionogi (SGIOY) (SGIOF) has increased its stake.
  • Under the agreement expected to close in Q1 2026, the New York-based pharma giant’s 11.7% stake in ViiV Healthcare will be replaced by an investment from Shionogi (SGIOY).
  • Once the new shareholding structure takes effect, Pfizer (PFE) will no longer have an equity interest in ViiV, Shionogi (SGIOF) will have its stake increased to 21.7%, and GSK (GSK) will retain its 78.3% ownership unchanged.
  • As part of the deal, ViiV Healthcare will issue new shares to Shionogi (SGIOF) for $2.125B and cancel Pfizer’s (PFE) holding in the company, generating $1.875M for the U.S.-based vaccine maker. Meanwhile, GSK (GSK) will be entitled to a special dividend of $0.250B payable in sterling pounds.

data.title: Wells Fargo's wealth management HQ said to move to West Palm Beach
data.description:
Wells Fargo (WFC) is relocating its wealth management headquarters to West Palm Beach, continuing a broader shift of financial firms to South Florida, according to a media report on Tuesday.

Bloomberg reported that the San Francisco-based company signed a lease with Related Ross to rent 50K square feet at the One Flagler office building, citing Wells Fargo wealth head Barry Sommers.

By the end of the year, some 100 people will be moved to the investment bank's new HQ, mostly comprising senior executives in the wealth business, Bloomberg reported. The office is set to open in August.

Wells Fargo (WFC) is joining a growing number of banks, such as JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Citizens Financial Group (CFG) in recent years expanding in South Florida for its tax advantages and strong tech and finance backdrop. 

More space might be rented out over time, Sommers said, Bloomberg reported, as the banks wants to attract financial advisers, as well as private bankers and independent broker-dealers.

Wells Fargo (WFC) didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: Centerra Gold upgraded at BMO as Kemess PEA boosts growth outlook
data.description:
Centerra Gold (CGAU) +11.2% in Tuesday's trading as precious metals futures soared to new all-time highs and BMO Capital upgraded shares to Outperform from Market Perform with a $32 price target, raised from $20, a day after the miner announced the results of a preliminary economic assessment for its Kemess gold-copper brownfield project in British Columbia.

The PEA outlined the potential for a hybrid open pit and underground mining operation with a 15-year life of mine starting in 2031; based on initial capital of $771M, the PEA results in an after-tax NPV5% of $1.1B and an internal rate of return of 16%, using respective long-term gold and copper prices of $3,000/oz and $4.50/lb.

"While in recent years the focus on Centerra has largely centered around operational consistency, free cash flow and capital returns, we believe Kemess offers a growth pivot that is significant for both institutional and strategic investors," BMO analyst Raj Ray wrote.

The analyst also noted Centerra's (CGAU) cash balance of $562M and no debt as of Q3 2025, and believes the company is in a strong position to fund all its growth projects internally, given current commodity prices.

data.title: Market Voices: Ray Dalio, Amazon CEO, Novartis CEO on Trump tariffs
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • Bridgewater Associates founder Ray Dalio said President Trump's aggressive foreign policy tactics could end up scaring off investors from buying U.S. assets.

“On the other side of trade deficits and trade wars, there are capital and capital wars,” Dalio told CNBC at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. “If you take the conflicts, you can’t ignore the possibility of the capital wars. In other words, maybe there’s not the same inclination to buy U.S. debt and so on.”

Dalio added he is worried that countries with significant holdings of U.S. dollars and Treasurys will be less inclined to finance American deficits amid escalating geopolitical tensions.

“We know that both the holders of U.S. dollars are denominated … and those who need it, the United States, are worried about each other. Right? So if you have other countries who are holding it, and they’re worried about each other, and we’re producing a lot of it, that’s a big issue,” Dalio said. 

  • Amazon (AMZN) CEO Andy Jassy said higher U.S. tariffs are beginning to be reflected in the prices of certain goods sold through its website.

Jassy attributed the rise to merchants exhausting inventories pre-purchased ahead of the tariffs.

“So you start to see some of the tariffs creep into some of the prices, some of the items, and you see some sellers are deciding that they’re passing on those higher costs to consumers in the form of higher prices, some are deciding that they’ll absorb it to drive demand, and some are doing something in between,” Jassy told CNBC at the Davos summit. “I think you’re starting to see more of that impact."

  • Novartis (NVS) CEO Vas Narasimhan said he believes the Swiss company's trade deal with the Trump administration, which calls for Novartis to invest $23B in U.S. operations, will likely shield it from U.S. tariffs on pharmaceuticals.

“We expect to be in a position by middle of this year where we are not really exposed to tariffs because we’re able to produce in the U.S. for the U.S. We have inventory on hand,” Narasimhan told CNBC at Davos. 

“We also have an agreement with the U.S. government that excludes us from any tariffs, we think, but in case that were not to be the case, we’re also future-proofed in the other direction as well,” Narasimhan added.

data.title: Trump's ideal pick for Fed chair becomes more elusive - report
data.description:
It's becoming clear that no one candidate will tick all the boxes for President Donald Trump's wish list for the next Federal Reserve chair, according to a media report on Tuesday. Furthermore, the Department of Justice's probe into current Fed Chair Jerome Powell raises questions about the central bank's independence, making the matter more complicated.

Trump wants a loyalist who will push the Federal Open Market Committee to significantly cut interest rates, who appeals to both the MAGA base and Wall Street, and who can clear a Senate confirmation, in addition to looking the part, Bloomberg News reported. He has become “somewhat frustrated” with his choices, the article said, citing people familiar with the matter.

The list of finalists stands at National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, BlackRock's (BLK) Rick Rieder, current Fed Governor Chris Waller, and former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh. Hassett had been considered the frontrunner until last week. Trump mused that his chief economic adviser was too valuable as a spokesperson for the administration's economic agenda. 

Prediction markets recently have Warsh as the favorite, with a 56% chance, followed by Rieder at 21%, according to Kalshi.

Rieder's odds of getting the post increased since Trump's remarks about Hassett last week.  He may have an easier time getting Senate confirmation and handily gets Wall Street approval, but the MAGA base may oppose him as a “globalist” banker.  Waller has an advantage in already being through the confirmation for his current role on the Fed board, but his eagerness to pursue Trump's reform of the central bank may be questioned. Meanwhile, Warsh isn't a known MAGA personality and doesn't have a dovish track record, Bloomberg said.

In an interview on Fox Business last week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who's running the Fed chair search, said, "What I can tell you is he's been very deliberate.  He has asked exactly the questions that I think you and I and the markets would want asked — who can bring the board along with them?  Who has the gravitas?. You know, everything you would want done."

Bessent also added that the administration wants someone with an "open Greenspan-like mind" that, in a productivity boom like the '90s, wouldn't "just throw the brakes on things because they're alarmed by a high GDP number."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
